title: Elliptic measures and the geometry of domains
speaker: Zihui Zhao
abstract: Given a bounded domain $\Omega$, the harmonic measure $\omega$ is a probability measure on $\partial \Omega$ and it characterizes where a Brownian traveller moving in $\Omega$ is likely to exit the domain from. The elliptic measure is a non-homogenous variant of harmonic measure. Since 1917, there has been much study about the relationship between the harmonic/elliptic measure $\omega$ and the surface measure $\sigma$ of the boundary. In particular, are $\omega$ and $\sigma$ absolutely continuous with each other?

proof_stages.label: Harmonic and elliptic measure setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines harmonic measure probabilistically and analytically, extends the definition to divergence-form elliptic operators, and formulates the basic comparison problem with boundary surface measure.

proof_stages.label: Harmonic-measure rectifiability paradigm
proof_stages.summary: Known results of Dahlberg and later qualitative and quantitative converses are reviewed to show that good behavior of harmonic measure is tied to rectifiability or uniform rectifiability of the boundary.

proof_stages.label: Elliptic coefficient obstruction and Kenig-Pipher control
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that rough elliptic matrices can destroy absolute continuity even in smooth domains, then states the Kenig-Pipher Carleson-type condition that restores A-infinity of elliptic measure in Lipschitz domains.

proof_stages.label: Main converse theorems for elliptic measure
proof_stages.summary: The main results are stated: under uniform-domain and Ahlfors-regular boundary assumptions, A-infinity of elliptic measure plus a Kenig-Pipher condition first with small constant and then with arbitrary finite constant implies uniform rectifiability of the boundary.

proof_stages.label: Dyadic cubes, stopping time, and sawtooth domains
proof_stages.summary: To pass from the small-constant theorem to the finite-constant theorem, the speaker introduces the Hofmann-Martell-Mayboroda extrapolation framework: dyadic cubes on a doubling boundary, local domains, bad-cube stopping times, and sawtooth subdomains avoiding bad regions.

proof_stages.label: Extrapolation criterion by fixed-portion removal
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reduced to showing that every cube with bounded Carleson ratio admits a collection of removed subcubes whose complement has uniformly rectifiable boundary and retains a fixed positive portion of the original surface measure.

proof_stages.label: Induction from small Carleson norm to arbitrary norm
proof_stages.summary: Starting from the small-norm theorem as the base case, an induction on multiples of epsilon constructs stopping-time families, separates good and bad cubes, applies the theorem on sawtooth regions and the inductive hypothesis on good children, and closes the packing estimate to prove the arbitrary-norm theorem.

examples.name: Four-corner Cantor set as bad topological boundary
examples.note: In the audience discussion, the four-corner Cantor set illustrates why rectifiability of the reduced boundary of a finite-perimeter set does not automatically control the whole topological boundary.

passes.reason: The four-corner Cantor set is invoked as a nonrectifiable topological boundary defeating the proposed rectifiability intuition.

The four-corner Cantor set is a purely unrectifiable fractal at the nonrectifiable extreme and is invoked to show the topological boundary need not be rectifiable.
The four-corner Cantor set is a totally disconnected fractal boundary with collapsed rectifiable structure, invoked to refute a naive rectifiability claim for topological boundaries.

examples.name: Unit ball with singular elliptic measure
examples.note: The speaker cites elliptic coefficient matrices on the unit ball whose elliptic measure is singular to surface measure, motivating why coefficient regularity is needed beyond geometric niceness of the domain.
examples.engagement_reason: the unit ball is cited as a domain where some elliptic matrix can give singular elliptic measure, but no matrix or calculation is worked out

passes.reason: The maximally symmetric unit ball is paired with rough coefficients to show singular elliptic measure can occur even in a smooth domain.

examples.name: Upper half-space Carleson calculation
examples.note: The upper half-space is used as a routine model case to unpack the Kenig-Pipher Carleson condition and show that the scaled gradient of the coefficient matrix must decay near the boundary.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the Carleson condition to the upper half-space, rewrites the integral with delta=t and denominator r^(n-1), and checks why a dt/t divergence forces decay

examples.name: Modulus-controlled singular coefficient matrices
examples.note: In response to an optimality question, the speaker describes known counterexample families where a coefficient matrix has a prescribed modulus of continuity but still produces singular elliptic measure when the relevant control fails.
